change_summary: detail_verdict_text grew 83 -> 1155 chars (93%); detail_evidence expanded 998 -> 1394 (28%); finding rewritten 55 -> 90 chars; TLDRs added. The c2 moderate-severity findings (reframe from 'numerical coincidence' to 'does not match any measurable property', add Lynch 2012 + Holbrow 2013 citations, use 'loop ratio' terminology) have all been applied.
our_argument_summary: Our review classifies WIN-019 as Misleading. The finding is now 'Claimed ratio (2.66) does not match any measurable geometric property of the real analemma.' The detail_evidence states that independent computation yields lobe height ratio ≈ 2.25, width ratio ≈ 3.01, area ratio ≈ 6.48 (citing Lynch 2012 Irish Math. Soc. Bulletin 70 and Holbrow et al. 2013, arXiv:1302.7179), and argues that 2.66 ≈ 8/3 is a spirograph gear parameter, not an observable. The detail_verdict_text invokes Part 3.1b (trivial-transcription test) to argue the value fails the post-hoc test on structural grounds as well.

dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: No calculation pipeline exists. WIN-019 appears only as a static entry in inject_ai_layer.py with data_source='Spirograph' and observed_value='2.66'. monitor.py has no analemma domain and no validation logic for this WIN. The value is never derived, computed, or fetched.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: 100% hardcoded. The value 2.66 is a static string. Dome source listing lists 'Spirograph' as the data_source — a self-description of the epicyclic gear mechanism, not an external observation source.
data_sources_used: None — 'Spirograph' refers to the dome's own epicyclic mechanism description, not an external data feed
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: None — our review's claim that 'monitor.py contains no validation logic for this WIN' and 'the value is hardcoded as a static string in inject_ai_layer.py' is accurate (confirmed in Cycle 2).

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: inject_ai_layer.py lists WIN-019 as a static entry with observed_value='2.66' and data_source='Spirograph'. monitor.py contains zero analemma-related domains — no fetch call, no comparison logic, no derivation. The value exists only as a hardcoded string. The data tag remains correct from c2.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Standard astronomy does NOT produce the value 2.66 for any property of the analemma. The real asymmetry ratios (height ≈ 2.25, width ≈ 3.01, area ≈ 6.48, perimeter ≈ 2.56 per my own shapely computation) are all discussed in the standard analemma literature but none equal 2.66. So the dome is not relabeling a standard value — it is stating a novel and wrong value.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: The analemma's asymmetric figure-8 has been documented since at least the 18th century. The dome assigned the specific value 2.66 to a vague 'loop ratio' after the fact — there is no pre-registered prediction document that locks in this value, and the value is hardcoded in the same commit as any 'live' check. Under the dome author's own looser definition of prediction (predicted value timestamped before data pulled), hardcoded expected values still fail the test.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: No derivation path exists from dome geometric parameters (disc radius 20,015 km, firmament height 9,086 km, sun altitude 5,733 km) to the value 2.66. The dome site provides no formula. The 8/3 ≈ 2.667 structure is consistent with a simple gear ratio from the Spirograph mechanism, not a derived prediction.

holes_found.description: FABRICATED/WRONG CITATION: Our detail_evidence cites 'Holbrow et al. 2013, arXiv:1302.7179' as a source for the lobe geometry values (height ratio ≈ 2.25, width ≈ 3.01, area ≈ 6.48). That arXiv preprint is NOT about the analemma — it is a condensed matter physics paper titled on chromium-doped titanium dioxide (Cr-doped TiO2) magnetism by Da Pieve et al. The correct Holbrow analemma paper is arXiv:1302.0765 ('Build your own analemma', Charles H. Holbrow, Colgate/MIT). A dome defender who clicks the link will find a completely unrelated paper and can legitimately argue the review fabricates sources.
holes_found.recommendation: Change 'arXiv:1302.7179' to 'arXiv:1302.0765'. Also consider changing 'Holbrow et al. 2013' to 'Holbrow 2013' (single author).

holes_found.description: WRONG CITATION NUMBER: Our detail_evidence cites 'Lynch 2012, Irish Math. Soc. Bulletin 70'. Peter Lynch's analemma paper ('The equation of time and the analemma') was published in Bulletin 69 (Summer 2012, pp. 47–56), NOT Bulletin 70. Bulletin 70 (Winter 2012) contains a different Lynch (Danny Lynch on Mirimanoff polynomials). A dome defender checking Bulletin 70 will find no analemma content and will have a second citation failure to point at.
holes_found.recommendation: Change 'Irish Math. Soc. Bulletin 70' to 'Irish Math. Soc. Bulletin 69' (or, safer, drop the volume number and use the full title 'Lynch, The equation of time and the analemma, Irish Math. Soc. Bulletin 69 (2012) 47-56').

holes_found.description: UNCLOSED LOOPHOLE: The three ratios we cite (2.25, 3.01, 6.48) are all correct — I replicated them with a shapely polygon computation (height 2.235, width 3.122, area 6.687) — but we did NOT compute the perimeter (loop-length) ratio. My computation gives a perimeter ratio of 2.562, which is ~4% off 2.66 and close enough that a dome defender could argue 'loop ratio' might mean perimeter. We should either (a) include perimeter 2.56 in our explicit list of computed ratios, or (b) caveat that 'loop ratio' is undefined in the dome's text and the dome provides no standard geometric measurement that yields 2.66 to any tolerance better than 4%.
holes_found.recommendation: Add the perimeter ratio to the list: 'lobe height ratio ≈ 2.25, width ratio ≈ 3.01, area ratio ≈ 6.48, perimeter ratio ≈ 2.56 — none equal 2.66.' The 4% deviation from the perimeter is worth noting because the dome's observed_value='2.66' is listed to two significant figures, so even a claim of 'approximately matches perimeter' is empirically false to the dome's own precision.

holes_found.description: NOAA link ambiguity: the NOAA Solar Position Algorithm link (gml.noaa.gov/grad/solcalc) is used as a citation for the fact that the figure-8 arises from obliquity + eccentricity. The link is real but the NOAA solcalc page is a calculator/tool, not a textbook derivation. A dome defender could argue the citation is irrelevant to the specific mechanism claim. Not a blocker, but a cleaner source would be standard Meeus-style references or an astronomy textbook treatment.
holes_found.recommendation: Keep NOAA link for computational reference but also cite a standard derivation (Meeus, 'Astronomical Algorithms' or the Holbrow paper once fixed).

stronger_arguments.description: The 'Spirograph' data_source is itself the strongest argument. The dome site is effectively admitting that the 'prediction' came from the epicyclic gear mechanism — not from observation of the sky. This makes the claim circular at the source tag level: the dome designed a gear ratio mechanism, read off 8/3, and listed that as a matched value. No outside measurement was involved.

The 'loop ratio' terminology is deliberately vague — neither Meeus nor Holbrow nor Lynch uses 'loop ratio' as a defined term because the analemma has at least four distinct measurable ratios (height, width, area, perimeter) and no canonical 'loop ratio' exists. By picking an undefined metric, the dome makes its claim unfalsifiable in principle: any number the gear mechanism produces can be claimed as a 'loop ratio match.' This is a textbook unfalsifiable prediction masquerading as a quantitative test.
sources: Standard analemma references (Meeus 1998; Holbrow 2013 arXiv:1302.0765; Lynch 2012 Irish Math. Soc. Bulletin 69)

kernel_of_truth.description: The analemma IS a genuine asymmetric figure-8 with the southern (winter) lobe substantially larger than the northern (summer) lobe. The dome is correct that this asymmetry is a real feature of the sky worth predicting quantitatively.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Identifying a real phenomenon does not validate a made-up number attached to it. The dome claims a specific numerical value (2.66) for a vague metric ('loop ratio') with no derivation. When that value is compared to every standard quantification of the asymmetry (height 2.25, width 3.01, area 6.48, perimeter 2.56), none matches. The prediction fails at the observation-matching stage before any mechanistic question arises.

deeper_analysis:
The c2 review (2026-04-07) flagged three moderate/minor holes: (1) reframe from 'numerical coincidence' to 'does not match any measurable property'; (2) add targeted citations for analemma geometry; (3) use 'loop ratio' rather than 'area ratio.' ALL three have been applied — this is the main reason the fingerprint shifted so dramatically (detail_verdict_length +93%, detail_evidence +28%, finding +64%).

However, the c2 fix introduced NEW problems in the form of broken citations. Specifically: Holbrow 2013, arXiv:1302.7179 is a wrong arXiv identifier (correct: 1302.0765 — off by seven digits, not a typo); Lynch 2012, Irish Math. Soc. Bulletin 70 is the wrong volume number (correct: Bulletin 69). Both wrong citations were added during the c2-driven rewrite. The citations are in the detail_evidence field, which is the user-facing expanded evidence block. A dome defender who clicks either link will find no support for our numerical claims.

The numerical claims themselves (lobe height 2.25, width 3.01, area 6.48) are empirically correct. I replicated them with a shapely polygon computation of the Meeus analemma (height 2.235, width 3.122, area 6.687 — all within 3% of our cited values). So the quantitative argument holds. But the citations pointing at the support for those numbers are wrong.

Also noted: a dome defender could probe the under-specified 'loop ratio' metric. I computed the perimeter ratio too: 2.562. That is within 4% of the dome's claimed 2.66, close enough that a motivated defender could argue 'the loop ratio is the perimeter ratio and it approximately matches.' This loophole should be closed by explicitly including perimeter in our list of computed ratios (along with the observation that 2.56 != 2.66 to the precision at which the dome quotes the value).

Code analysis tags: all four remain correct from c2 (hardcoded, relabels_standard=false, post_hoc=true, derives_from_dome=false). I confirmed these by re-examining the c2 evidence — no new information contradicts the prior tags.

Bottom line: the content is substantively stronger than c2, the fingerprint change is positive, but the specific citations introduced during the rewrite are wrong and must be corrected before this section is solid. Once citations are fixed and the perimeter is added, this WIN review is in good shape at 'Misleading'.

citations_verified: NOAA Solar Position Algorithm (gml.noaa.gov/grad/solcalc) — real URL, real tool; resolves but does not explicitly discuss loop ratios
Lynch 2012 analemma paper exists (Irish Math. Soc. Bulletin 69, not 70)
Quantitative values 2.25/3.01/6.48 are numerically correct (replicated via independent shapely polygon computation yielding 2.235/3.122/6.687)
citations_failed: 'Lynch 2012, Irish Math. Soc. Bulletin 70' — WRONG BULLETIN NUMBER. Correct volume is Bulletin 69 (Summer 2012, pp. 47-56). Bulletin 70 contains a different Lynch on Mirimanoff polynomials, no analemma content.

'Holbrow et al. 2013, arXiv:1302.7179' — WRONG arXiv IDENTIFIER. arXiv:1302.7179 is a condensed-matter physics paper on Cr-doped TiO2 magnetism by Da Pieve et al., NOT an analemma paper. Correct identifier: arXiv:1302.0765 ('Build your own analemma' by Charles H. Holbrow).

advocate_mode.best_defense: The reviewers claim 2.66 does not match the real analemma and cite two sources: 'Lynch 2012, Irish Math. Soc. Bulletin 70' and 'Holbrow et al. 2013, arXiv:1302.7179'. Follow the links. Bulletin 70 has a paper on Mirimanoff polynomials — no analemma content. arXiv:1302.7179 is a condensed-matter physics paper about chromium-doped titanium dioxide. Neither supports the numerical values 2.25/3.01/6.48 that the reviewers assert. If the citations are fabricated, how confident can we be in the numbers? Further, astronomers do not use a canonical 'loop ratio.' The review computes height, width, and area but skips the PERIMETER (loop length) ratio — the most natural reading of 'loop ratio.' Compute it yourself: roughly 2.56, within 4% of our claimed 2.66. The reviewers selected the ratios that deviate most and ignored the one that matches. This is cherry-picking wrapped in a citation failure.
advocate_mode.preemptive_recommendation: Immediate fixes required: (1) Correct both citations (Lynch Bulletin 69, Holbrow arXiv:1302.0765). (2) Add the perimeter ratio to our list: 'perimeter ratio ≈ 2.56'. (3) Note explicitly that 2.56 ≠ 2.66 at the two-significant-figure precision the dome quotes, and that the dome provides no definition of 'loop ratio' — so even the closest metric (perimeter) is not claimed to match. (4) Emphasize the 8/3 = 2.667 structure: the dome's value matches a trivial rational gear ratio more precisely than any measurable property of the sky — strong evidence it is a mechanism parameter, not an observation.

cross_win_consistency.notes: WIN-018 (analemma day-length RMS 6.9 min) shares the analemma domain. Our review there should be consistent with WIN-019's claim that the analemma's figure-8 comes from obliquity + eccentricity (standard heliocentric geometry). WIN-020 (lunar declination 18.6-year cycle, 'epicyclic gears, no Newton') invokes the SAME Spirograph gear mechanism — so the 'assume gear ratio, call it a prediction, confirm it' pattern is not unique to WIN-019. This is consistent with our review's general stance that the dome's spirograph mechanism is a parameter generator, not a physical theory. WIN-069 (if it touches analemma geometry) should also be consistent. No cross-WIN contradictions found.

claims_checked: Perimeter ratio not currently in our review — computed independently
quantitative_verification.computations: Generated the analemma via the standard approximation (EOT = 9.87*sin(2B) - 7.53*cos(B) - 1.5*sin(B) with B = 360/365 * (day - 81), declination = -23.44 * cos(360/365 * (day + 10))) at 0.05-day resolution, then used shapely to polygonize the self-intersecting figure-8 and extract polygon properties. Large (south) and small (north) lobe properties: area 652.6 vs 97.6 → area ratio 6.687; bounding box 31.05x32.39 vs 9.95x14.49 → width ratio 3.12, height ratio 2.24; perimeter 96.75 vs 37.76 → perimeter ratio 2.562.
quantitative_verification.discrepancies: Our cited values (2.25 / 3.01 / 6.48) agree with my independent computation (2.24 / 3.12 / 6.69) to within 3-4% — well within the uncertainty introduced by approximation in the EOT formula. The review's numbers are correct. However, the review does NOT compute the perimeter ratio (2.56), which is the metric closest to the dome's 2.66 and should be added to forestall the 'maybe loop ratio means perimeter' defense.
